speaker: interviewer
text: OK, this is tape five. We're interviewing Rena Gelissen. it's January 24, 1996. We left off with the last tape, you were selected by Mengele for what you think are sterilization experiments.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the reason that I saw that person, she's an important person, taking one person out. She must-- she must know it's-- it's very detailed, what that-- that you can save your life but just as you can lose your life maybe.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said to Danka, we are going. And she said no, I'm afraid. There were everywhere, [? SSes. ?] I said, what difference does it make? I said, they're going to kill us anyway. If you go in for sterilization, then they-- they're-- they're-- you're not going live anyway, I said. And you're going to be a cripple before that and suffer. I said, you going with me?

speaker: interviewee
text: And she didn't say anything. So I grabbed her hand and pretended I'm a very important person, holding her hand. The SS running around and counting there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went in back off the SS, went to the building where we changed the clothes, found the clothes, the whole pile of clothes that we-- that morning, that they changed us. And the aprons thrown off, put it on the board, and found there about 50 uniforms, I found Danka's and mine, which has the number on it. So it had to be the same thing, Danka's dress and mine. And run outside the back door again, in back of people. And they were lined up. And I asked, would you push up-- move up a little bit? And they started counting all over again. Because when one moves up, the other one move up and the whole row had to move up, or the whole-- the whole camp would.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they didn't know it to was. And to this day, I don't know who saved my life, the person that-- that came to pick out that one person saved our lives. Because this was, I found out later, sterilization.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And sterilization meant death after suffering.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Right. Mengele chose you for another detail later?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Later on, after so many-- so many selections, other ones, he came and chose again, for another detail. And there was no choice. He chose 50-- 50 of us. Danka was among the 50. He-- we did not go in, in the building, for quarantine. 50 of us are marching out, have no idea where we're going. And most of the time, I was thinking this is for death, already too, for whatever. And then, we came into a building. And it was the headquarters of the SS. And we ended up in the SS laundry, which saved-- actually, definitely saved our lives. Because in that laundry we-- even if we worked outside, there were no electric wires. There were no guards with the machines guns in there. And the roll call was happening inside. Food was exactly the same, very little and starvation diet. But the circumstances were better. There was not an SS everywhere with a rifle running around. There were no dogs, that we were terrified of it. And-- and there was-- there was no wires around-- walls around the-- around the camp. This was one building going behind an SS kitchen to another building in the back of the building were clothes lines. And I was chosen. The SS woman asked who speaks German. And I said, I do. And she chose me to hang out the clothes, outside. And with hanging out the clothes outside, and with living there and roll call on the inside, and there was one shower that once a month we could take a shower. The first time after-- this is two years later, almost two years later. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this helped a little bit. The food was the same. The portions were small. But there was no-- there were no beatings. If you did something wrong, there could have been. So if they would catch us with something doing wrong, there would have been beatings. And it could have been back to Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when you were sent to the laundry? Do you-- do you have any idea of date?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes there was a-- a-- a-- also October-- October, 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you work in the laundry?

speaker: interviewee
text: We worked in the laundry all the day till the death march.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. yeah. We worked in the laundry, in the staff quarters. They had two floors up, and we were in the basement, always working. And then suddenly, there were rumors that the Russians are coming closer, and they're going to lose the war. The Germans are going to lose the war. And when a couple weeks later, they moved us. They move their-- their-- I don't know where their people were, the officers where they went, from the two floors above us. But we were moved to a-- a leather factory, and to new blocks, they called. Again, that we were sifting sand. Remember, that was for new blocks, that they're building more of them. We moved into the new blocks. And in the morning an-- an SS woman was taking us out to go-- you see, in first laundry, there was an SS woman. Because there was just near the-- that-- the drying place, the truck, that was near. But this one was farther, to the leather factory. And they took us out there every single day. And one of the days they were taking us out there, when it-- when it was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we worked almost a year there. But in 1945, we heard some rumors that there were some-- we heard some rumors they're going to blow up a-- a crematorium. And one date in October, I think a '45, they-- this is a

speaker: interviewer
text: '44. --year --year later. No, this is-- [? God. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: No, '44, sorry. '44, the crematorium was a big detonation we had. And that was supposed to save their lives, the men that worked in the sonderkommando. But anyway, it's a long story. Four Jewish girls participated in it. And they were arrested. And we were first very all joyful that the crematoriums are going to be broken up. But then when we had the four girls who were arrested. And they going to be hanged. And the SS woman took us to the camp, to Birkenau, again. We're are not in Birkenau. Those new blocks are not in Birkenau either. That's a separate camp again, without so much wires and-- and walls than-- than it was in Birkenau and in Auschwitz I.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went to Birkenau, and had to lineup. And orders were really shouting loud, the-- the-- the-- the officers, straight looking at the girls. We had to look where the girls are going to be hanged. And the four girls were marched in.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they put the-- stepped on the chairs. And they were singing Hebrew songs. And-- [INAUDIBLE] OK. And then we had to keep the eyes open, otherwise we're going to be shot. And then the SS woman brought us back to the-- to our quarters, where we-- to the laundry again after they-- they killed the-- hanged the girls. And-- and she made us sing German songs on the way home, on the way there, and on the way back, to the laundry mat. And that was the SS laundry. And then we heard rumors again, the Russians are really coming close. And then one day, the-- the-- from the men's camp, I received a letter. I had on the-- on the drying place-- I met him in that-- that worked in the men's commando. And he sent me a note with the people that brought the tea. From the men's camp, the people who brought in from the kitchen, the SS-- from the camp kitchen, they were bringing to the women's camp, the tea and the bread. And he-- he send me a note, that Jewish boy, that they're going to march out-- march out people. They already started. And this was like, the 16 I think, or something, or the 17. So I received a note that he's going to send me some-- to send him a note back with these people and to tell him how many people I have in my group that I like, aside-- in addition to Danka and me. And I told him six of us. And he sent pants and shoes and socks and six loafs of bread and some cubes of sugar. He sent to it, with the kettle, when the kettle was empty. Instead of the tea in it, they risked their life. Those-- those were gentiles, to us, that brought in the tea-- risked their lives to having that kettle, the food and the clothes for us. And then on-- And then we-- we marched out on the death march. On January 18, one o'clock at night in January. He also sent a watch with him that I have in my memorabilia, I have from [? Eric, ?] that watch. And we started marching. And sometimes the-- we heard the detonations from the Russians coming. But we had no choice. They dragged us along. And the sick could stay behind. And you could hide-- maybe hide with the sick staying behind. But then rumors were going they're going to set a fire to the camp from all sides. So you-- you couldn't win either way.

speaker: interviewer
text: So while we was marching, death, the-- there was dead people. It was the worst snowstorm in many, many years. So we're marching in the deep snow without food, without drink, only what we had from the-- the-- in two days, the food was gone. What we had, those six loaves of bread, we just divided it, and it was gone.

speaker: interviewee
text: At once in a while, they let us stop in the barn in the-- in the Polish farmers. This is Poland that we are going to. Because Auschwitz is Poland too. We're going towards the German border. And-- and on one of those trips, I-- I started getting sore feet, had blisters on the feet and I had diarrhea. And I was really getting it. I said after Auschwitz, to have this one, and it doesn't look like we're going to be alive. Because if you just slipped or something or went out of line, you already got the bullet. And when they fell-- fell down, you don't know if they're dead, how they're going to be suffered there. So I decided that I saw, a day before, a girl go into an outhouse.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know how farmers have an outhouse? Go in, and she-- we were already lined up. And she didn't come out. So the SS opened up the door and saw her there and gave-- shot her. And then I saw her roll out of that. I decided I-- it seems to me like an easier death than walking the death march. So I was going to abandon Danka and I went in.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then before I had a chance to do it, she and another friend from the group of six walked by that, opened the door and dragged me out, dragged me of there. And weren't supposed to hold anybody or support. But they supported me. Under the elbows, they were holding me. And like, two hours or three hours later, I was a different person. The feet weren't hurting so much. I have no idea why. Maybe the encouragement of Danka being so terrified, and the  other friend, and I was going to abandon her. And I suddenly realized I made a promise and I'm not keeping it. So I almost broke it. And so I went along and I felt better. And then we were put into trains. And from the trains, they dragged us into-- in the middle of nowhere. And then we walked again, three hours, that next day or the third day. I don't remember what it was. And we ended up in Ravensbruck. That was all full of women they had some brought in from the Warsaw uprising, some woman that were fighting in that uprising. So all the beds, everything, was theirs. So we had-- on the dirt floor we were sleeping that night, and a couple of nights. And there was practically no food. But a week later, they came to pick us up with-- with military trucks again and brought us to last camp, to Neustadt-Glewe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you liberated from that camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were liberated from that camp by the 82nd-- I just recently found out-- by the 82nd Airborne Division, stationed right there in North Carolina, where I live now. So then-- then we were liberated. And the Russians came at the same-- the same time too, there. Then, they formed the-- Ludwigslust is a bigger city. And [? therefore, ?] where the Americans pulled back five miles. And in-- and it was no-man's land in between. And the Russians stayed behind.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we decided instead of going home, what I promised my mom, I finally realized or allowed myself to realize, that the promise that I made, that I'm going to bring her home to mama, mama's going to open the door, I say, I brought your baby back, that this is not going to happen. I said, why don't we just go?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ten of us decided to try to get-- there was already a soldier there, checking for passes. But we passed by the soldier by telling him some story that I made up. And we went to the other side. And-- and we went to the Americans. And then the Americans wouldn't let us in because they didn't know who we were. We didn't speak the English language. Only one of us spoke. And they thought we may be spies or something, so they had--

speaker: interviewer
text: So they went to get the major and the major brought us into a big beautiful villa, a mansion. And the owners of the villa, of the mansion were gone. And just the housekeeper was there.

speaker: interviewee
text: They ordered the housekeeper, he's going to bring us bread and cheese. He's going to bring us everything that we need. And she's supposed to serve us breakfast in bed. So we stayed there a couple of days there, in that mansion. And then we had to go to the displaced persons camp because near Ludwigslust were about 20 little camps around there. And Americans were getting crazy, to have different people anywhere. He said, I can't do it any longer, have you here. We have to be concentrated in one building and bringing you where you want to. So he made sure that-- that one of the [INAUDIBLE] brought us to the Netherlands.

speaker: interviewer
text: So were you in a DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was a empty school building.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK? During the war, they-- they-- I guess they weren't going to the school there. And-- and--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Especially when the Allies were marching in. So the Germans left the school building there, in Ludwigslust. So the school building was used for us, that we slept there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me how you met your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They brought us to-- to the Netherlands. We decided among us that we'd go to the Netherlands, the six of us. The others ones from the other four that were there, they decide something else, to go to France, I think. And when the six of us decided to go, then they dropped us off at a hospital. Because they-- at the border, they did not-- they did not want to let us in if we had any contagious-- contagious diseases or any serious diseases.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they kept us for three days in quarantine at the hospital and check our lungs and everything. And miracle above miracles, everything was OK. All six of us were let into the country. And first they were going to send us back to Germany. But that's a long story. And I begged the major not to send us. I said, why don't you just kill us if you're going to send us back to Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had enough of it. So he said, I don't know what to do with you. He said, I'll tell you what I'll do, you're begging so much. My-- my cars from here, my military trucks are bringing in gentile prisoners of war that they took-- not prisoners of war-- they took, people, Dutch people, civilians they took while they were fighting on the front and losing a lot of people. They replaced them with Dutch people, but taken by force. And they were returning. So I'm going to just sneak you in among that. And when we came in, there were relief teams. And John Gelissen was the head of on of the relief teams, the Captain of it. And we came into his camp and then he processed the other people. And the-- the bridges-- there's a lot of war there in the Netherlands. And there were a lot of the bridges broken. So he had to-- he had to disinfect the people, feed them, and then give them transportation to bring them to their homes. But they had passports. They had papers. They were citizens of there, and they were gentiles. And he didn't know what to do with five girls, five-- five of us there. One of them we lost in-- in the military compound. We didn't know where she was. And she later got herself to France, and she lives in France.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the five of us came into his camp. So after we were there a whole week, he said what are we going to do? They have no papers, no identification. So he took us to the police station and registered us, by names, what we told them, who we were. And he put his name down and signed that he is-- he is guaranteeing for-- vouching for us, for the five Jewish girls there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we stayed in the Red Cross. And he employed us, asked for the talents, what we have. So I said, I know how to sew. So Danka and I did sewing. Two of them did dusting. And one worked in the kitchen.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as long as the-- as the camp lasted, the people were coming back from Germany. He was-- he was still there. And we were helping there. And we were getting a salary too, from the Allies, from either the British or the-- or Canadians, I think. When did you marry?

speaker: interviewee
text: We started-- he started being very nice us, bringing us back. He brought us back to the normal world again. We were-- we were hiding bread in a suitcase in the-- everything that's left over from food, we were helping with the cleaning up after the meals too, we were hiding in a suitcase. And one of the nurses there-- he had a staff of people. He had people working from him, nurses and chauffeurs. They were driving the people back with the cars that he had, to their homes. And one of them found out and went to the commander. He was the commander of the team and told him. And he-- he took-- made all the arrangements with me. Because I was the only one that spoke German, again.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he-- and while we were speaking that, we were talking about other things too. He started flirting with me. And he decided there into-- into socializing with other Red Cross camps in the neighborhood. And they had dances.

speaker: interviewee
text: He decided to take us to the dances there too. And he brought us into life again, and treating us like ladies. And he was the gentleman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Again, when did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, two years later--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: When the-- when the camps were over, we went-- he came and picked us up. We were going first to [INAUDIBLE], to-- after the camps were over, he-- he brought us with his trucks and cars, brought us to it. He found out that in one city was a building. And-- and there were Jewish people, survivors, that came back, are working there. And they were working just like [INAUDIBLE], in Israel. And they are waiting to get to Israel, when the borders are free, when the British are going to give up and-- and not arrest everybody that's going there. And then John came back. And on one day he pretended that he came with a-- a friend of his, by a motorcycle, went for business there. And he came by with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just-- and so he came to talk to me. And then the next time, when he came, he said he would want us to come there. Why do we stay here?

speaker: interviewee
text: And his cook is going to take us into his house. And he took us there into his house. And then we got to know each other better. And two years later, we were married. In '47.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. When did you come to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came in 1954.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, four children. The oldest daughter is Sylvia. And the second one is Joseph. And the third one is Peter. And the fourth one is Robert, the only who is born in the United States and he can become president. The other ones can't.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What kinds of occupations did you have when you came to this country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had occupation being housewife and mother for the four-- for the three children. And I had three at that time. One of them was born in the United States. And John was a-- a-- a physical education professor, at college, Professor. He already taught-- he already taught physical education in Europe. But because the physical education in Europe is completely different position, when we came to America, they wanted him to be a coach. And he was going to travel all over the country with the-- with the-- with the people. And he didn't want to do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he went-- took courses at-- in-- I never remember the university. At the university there, took courses, while he got the position as a teacher, as a professor in the Biddeford, Maine. At the same time, he was traveling to Massachusetts to-- to study, and to change that into-- plus he had, already physical education, a lot of biology.

speaker: interviewee
text: He started change that. And he studied biology. And he graduated and-- and was, since then, a biology professor. And he taught German and Latin too, by the way. He was qualified to teach those subjects. I shouldn't cut it short. I wanted to brag about him.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: If you don't mind.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he deserves it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know you wrote a book.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're doing an interview with us now. Can you tell me why you're doing-- you wrote the book, why you're doing the interview? Is there some messages that you want to give--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I do.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I promised myself when I was still in Auschwitz, until-- in-- in Auschwitz, I already started thinking about it. As I told you, from a little town, I was naive. So I had-- my way of thinking was a little bit different than the world is like.

speaker: interviewer
text: I promised myself when I come out, I'm going to tell the world what happened, that they were throwing in children into the gas chambers and into the fires, and what they did. And maybe if I write a-- a book, and maybe some people are going to find out, there's never going to be a war again. If they hear what has happened, how could they have a war again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this didn't happen. Because people didn't listen very much for 50 years. But I had that message. And I wanted to give it anyway, now. And I wasn't very-- I-- I-- I gave up, already, actually. But then I met a friend that has another friend that could write. And she told me, you were in Auschwitz. You should write a book about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said I didn't find anybody that cared. I have just the person for you. And that was Heather Macadam, the person. I said-- I said, how old is that person? 32. I said, oh come on. She's a baby. What does she know about Jews and about the Holocaust in Europe? She lives in America, born in America.

speaker: interviewee
text: I promise you, she knows everything about it. She happens to be very much interested in Holocaust history. I said OK, we meet. And we met. And-- and the book was written to honor my parents, my parent's memory. I got to watch it now, because the tears come. And-- and to speak for six million Jews that can't speak for themselves. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is--

speaker: interviewee
text: This was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there any message that you'd like to give for your children and your grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: To spread the word, what happened, so it never happens again. Maybe, just maybe. Seeing what all goes on, I don't know how much I accomplish with it. But if I just accomplish a little bit, I'm going to be happy with it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: (WHISPERS) Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, Mrs. Gelissen, would you please introduce us to your family here?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my oldest daughter, Sylvia, next to me. And next to her is my husband and her father, John Gelissen.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sylvia, was there something you wanted to say? SYLVIA (DAUGHTER): Yes, that I'm very happy to say, at the least, that my mother made it through this experience. Because otherwise I wouldn't be here, nor my three brothers, nor my two nephews, nor my niece. And her surviving has also acted as a situation that I can follow in my own life, when I need strength and courage. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Czechoslovakia, I think, when-- when she was married. I don't know if it was [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell us, what's this picture now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, OK. This is my beautiful mama. And I think the picture was taken where she was born in Petrova, Czechoslovakia. And either a wedding picture or right after the wedding. Because she looks very young.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is of the family and made especially for my older sister that was in-- in the US at that time. And Mama wanted to feel that she is at home, to see the picture. From left to right is the youngest sister, Danka. Then it's Mama. And then it's--

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother's name? First name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sara. And then it's the second sister after the one that is in America, Gertrude, is Zosia. And she was 14 years older than I was. And-- and Gertrude, 16 years older. And The next is Papa, next to the older sister. And then the last one the right-hand side, that's myself, Rena.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know when this was taken? Or approximately when?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is-- I was about six years old. And I'm 75 now. So you want to do the figuring or should I?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where it was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in-- in-- in a resort-- we had to drive there, to the photographer-- in Krynica.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is of my hometown, Tylicz, and located in the Carpathian Mountains, in the south of Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is approximately third grade picture for Danka, for my sister, Danka. And her cousin is standing right next to her, Sylvia. And they were the same age, I think. And I was in fifth grade then and I think I was standing somewhere in back. I'm not visible. And the two teachers, the [? lay ?] teacher and the nun.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In my hometown, Tylicz. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: (WHISPERS) Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the middle of the picture, is-- on the left side, my cousin Sylvia Kornreich. And on the right side is Danka Kornreich, my sister. And it's taken at school, in Tylicz. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is again, the same as the other picture. Only they allowed-- the teachers allowed the boys to be in it too. And it's again, Danka and Sylvia, and more to the right. The second--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the right-hand side is my sister, Danka, my youngest sister. And next to her, my cousin, Sylvia. And what's this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is myself, on the right-hand side, about 16. And on the left-hand side, Danka, my younger sister. And the middle is Dina Goldberger, and she lives in France. of if us survived Auschwitz. And the bear is a man dressed as a bear to earn some money in the winter months. And-- and his buddy take-- took the picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who's this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is friend of mine, a childhood friend. He is here now, a grown up. He was a pilot. And later on during the war, he was a-- a-- working for the underground. And he saved my life by bringing me over from Poland, from danger to Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's from my hometown, Tylicz. And his name was Andrej Garbera. He died on this job, doing the underground work.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of myself-- you can see by this sad eyes-- after I survived Auschwitz, about three months later, taken in the Netherlands, when we came in after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was it taken?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my sister, Danka and I, about six months, or almost a year, I think. So it's in 1946, maybe. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And who's on the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the left is my younger sister, Danka. And we both had new dresses. So we made a nice picture about it. And we recovered from Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's you on the right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I am on the right. Sorry. And this is myself--

speaker: interviewer
text: What's this?

speaker: interviewee
text: --on the right. And on the left, John Gelissen. This is our wedding picture. On July 29, 1947. I almost forgot. 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, this picture is the last picture before we came to the United States. We came to the-- to US in 1954. And this is with the three children, Sylvia, on the left side with her doll, Joseph in the middle, and Peter in my arms. And my husband in the background.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a family picture taken with children-- our children and spouses. And it's taken, I think, to honor the 75th birthday of my husband, John Gelissen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And let's see, it was the-- yes, it was the 75th birthday. And next to him is the-- our son-in-law, Jim, and our daughter, Sylvia. And then it's-- is the baby there? Do you see the baby there?

speaker: interviewee
text: The baby granddaughter, Julia, and myself in the center. And then Julia's mother, my daughter-in-law, Claire. And her husband, my son, Robert. And then my other daughter-in-law, [? Laurie. ?] And at the end of it, my son, Peter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my book, a dream come true-- a 50-years dream come true. I was dreaming about to write down whatever-- what happened to us during World War II, and my family. And the cover picture there is-- and the cover picture is in the center, my older sister. And at the right of her, my father. And at the left of her, my mother and my younger-- and next to my mother, my younger sister, Danka. In the right-hand side, in the corner is myself, Rena.
